items.q: Is building on brownfield sites always better than greenfield?
items.correct: Not always — brownfield reuses urban land and existing services, but sites can be costly to decontaminate and too small, so some greenfield building may still be needed.
items.random_wrong: Brownfield sites are fields where brown crops are grown.
items.why: Students learn 'brownfield = good' as a slogan; evaluation needs the cost and capacity drawbacks too.

items.q: Does the green belt stop all building around cities?
items.correct: No — it strongly restricts development, but exceptions exist and land can be released; it can also push development beyond the belt, lengthening commutes.
items.misconception: Green belt land can never be built on.
items.random_wrong: The green belt is a ring road painted green.
items.why: Students treat the policy as absolute; the leapfrogging effect is a key evaluative point in UK challenges.

items.q: Could the UK switch entirely to wind and solar power today?
items.correct: Not yet — wind and solar are intermittent, so the energy mix still needs storage, nuclear or gas backup while grid and battery technology develop.
items.misconception: The UK could run on 100% wind and solar tomorrow without problems.
items.random_wrong: Renewable energy only works in the summer months.
items.why: Intermittency is the standard challenge to naive all-renewables answers about the UK energy mix.

items.correct: Land that has previously been developed, often in urban areas
items.random_wrong: Farmland that has never been built on
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Land that has previously been developed, often in urban areas. “Protected countryside around a city” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The UK's legally binding net zero emissions target year is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2050. “2030” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Green belt policy is designed to:
items.correct: Prevent urban sprawl by restricting development around cities
items.why: It's Prevent urban sprawl by restricting development around cities. “Increase parking in city centres” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which trend increases pressure on UK housing supply?
items.correct: A rising population and more single-person households
items.random_wrong: Shrinking demand for homes in the south-east
items.why: It's A rising population and more single-person households. “A rapidly falling population” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If the UK population grows from 60 million to 67 million, what is the percentage increase, to one decimal place?
items.why: Exact answer: 11.7 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
